title: Always wrap errors with context
description: Bare error returns lose the call chain context. Use fmt.Errorf with %w to wrap errors so the full trace is preserved for debugging.

title: Never ignore errors
description: Every error return must be checked. Ignoring errors leads to silent failures that are extremely hard to debug in production.

title: No panic in library or service code
description: panic should never be used for recoverable error conditions in service or library code. It crashes the entire process and cannot be handled gracefully by callers. Use error returns instead.

title: Context must be the first parameter
description: Functions that accept a context.Context must have it as their first parameter, named ctx. This is a universal Go convention.

title: Never store context in a struct
description: context.Context should be passed as a function parameter, never stored in a struct field. Contexts are request-scoped and storing them leads to lifecycle bugs.

title: Use structured logging only
description: All logging must use structured logging (slog, zap, zerolog). Never use fmt.Println, fmt.Printf, or log.Println for application logging.

title: No fmt.Println for logging
description: fmt.Println writes to stdout without timestamps, levels, or structured fields. It cannot be filtered, sampled, or enriched with context. Use slog or your team's structured logger.
violation_example: fmt.Println("Payment processed:", result.ID)
correct_example: logger.Info("payment processed", "payment_id", result.ID)

title: No hardcoded secrets or credentials
description: Never hardcode passwords, API keys, tokens, or secrets in source code. Use environment variables, secret managers (Vault, AWS Secrets Manager), or config files excluded from VCS.
violation_example:
const apiKey = "sk-abc123secretkey"  // BAD
var dbPassword = "admin123"            // BAD

title: Use parameterized queries for SQL
description: Never construct SQL queries with string concatenation or fmt.Sprintf. Always use parameterized queries to prevent SQL injection.
violation_example:
query := fmt.Sprintf("SELECT * FROM users WHERE id = '%s'", userID)  // BAD: SQL injection
rows, err := db.Query(query)
correct_example: rows, err := db.QueryContext(ctx, "SELECT * FROM users WHERE id = $1", userID)  // GOOD: parameterized

title: JWT secret must come from environment or secret manager
description: JWT signing secrets must never be hardcoded. They must be loaded from environment variables or a secret management system.
violation_example:
secret := "mysupersecret123"
token := jwt.NewWithClaims(jwt.SigningMethodHS256, claims)
tokenString, _ := token.SignedString([]byte(secret))

title: Use MixedCaps for Go names, not underscores
description: Go uses MixedCaps (camelCase/PascalCase) for all names. Underscores are not idiomatic except in test function names (Test_xxx) and cgo.
violation_example:
var user_name string      // BAD
func get_user_by_id() {}  // BAD
correct_example:
var userName string       // GOOD
func getUserByID() {}     // GOOD

title: Acronyms should be all caps
description: Common acronyms like URL, HTTP, ID, API, JSON should be all uppercase or all lowercase, never mixed. Use userID not userId, httpClient not httpClient.
violation_example:
type HttpClient struct {}  // BAD: Http → HTTP
var usrId int              // BAD: Id → ID
correct_example:
type HTTPClient struct {}  // GOOD
var userID int             // GOOD

title: Goroutines must have a clear termination path
description: Every goroutine launched must have a clear mechanism to stop (context cancellation, done channel, WaitGroup). Goroutine leaks are silent memory/CPU leaks.

title: Use directional channels when possible
description: Function parameters should use directional channel types (chan<- T or <-chan T) to express intent and prevent misuse at compile time.

title: Use table-driven tests
description: Tests with multiple cases should use the table-driven pattern with subtests. This makes tests readable, easy to extend, and provides clear failure messages.

title: Repository function must contain exactly one database call
description: Each repository function must perform exactly one database operation. Multiple DB calls in a single repository function blur responsibilities, make transactions ambiguous, and hide N+1 patterns. Split the function or wrap the calls in a single transaction helper.

title: Database call must not appear inside a for/range loop (N+1 anti-pattern)
description: Issuing a database call inside a loop produces N round-trips for N iterations (the N+1 query problem). This causes exponential latency growth and connection-pool exhaustion under load. Batch the identifiers and issue a single bulk query instead.

title: Handler must not call more than one repository method directly
description: HTTP handlers should be thin adapters. Orchestrating multiple repository calls directly in a handler embeds business logic in the transport layer, prevents reuse, complicates testing, and leaks domain concerns. Introduce a service/use-case layer to own the multi-repo orchestration.

title: Pre-allocate slices when size is known
description: When the final size of a slice is known or can be estimated, use make([]T, 0, n) to pre-allocate. This avoids repeated allocations and copying during growth.

title: Exported functions must have doc comments
description: Every exported function, type, constant, and variable must have a doc comment starting with the name of the element. This is required by godoc and golint.
